danger_signals.visual_form: opposing aircraft rendered in flight view during Europe 1917 mode
danger_signals.meaning: active combat threat when war is declared

danger_signals.visual_form: mountains on two sides of grid in Europe 1917 mode
danger_signals.meaning: environmental hazard requiring navigation skill to avoid

safety_signals.visual_form: Meigs Field in Chicago rendered as starting point with city skyline and Lake Michigan visible
safety_signals.meaning: safe landing zone and reference landmark for navigation

safety_signals.visual_form: city skylines (Chicago, Los Angeles, New York City, Seattle) visible from flight view
safety_signals.meaning: safe navigation landmarks and world orientation cues

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Sopwith Camel aircraft in Europe 1917 mode
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: grid-divided terrain with mountains in Europe 1917 combat zone

faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: enemy aircraft rendered distinctly in flight view during combat

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: clear out-the-window display showing US regional terrain (Chicago, Los Angeles, New York, Seattle) with city skylines, lakes, and landscape features; variable weather and time of day conditions
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player can freely navigate and explore without combat threat

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: grid-divided terrain area with mountains on two sides; enemy aircraft visible in flight space; Cessna replaced with Sopwith Camel aircraft
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: player can declare war and engage in combat with enemy aircraft; navigation constrained by terrain hazards

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: dynamic weather conditions affecting visibility and rendering of terrain and sky
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: flight difficulty and visibility conditions change based on weather system

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: lighting conditions and sky color change to reflect different times of day
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: visual navigation cues shift with daylight/darkness conditions

environmental_storytelling.example: real US cities (Chicago, Los Angeles, New York City, Seattle) rendered with recognizable skylines and landmarks; Meigs Field as default airport grounds player in authentic aviation history
Europe 1917 mode communicates historical context through aircraft type (Sopwith Camel), grid terrain layout, and mountain barriers suggesting WWI flight combat environment
Lake Michigan visibility from Chicago starting point; city skylines as visual references for orientation and position verification

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: responds to control inputs; rendered in first-person out-the-window view
can fire at enemy aircraft when war is declared in Europe 1917 mode
visible in flight space after war declaration; can be engaged with player fire

notes: Microsoft Flight Simulator (1982/4.0) uses minimal visual communication due to early PC hardware constraints (monochrome/limited color capability). World-state is communicated primarily through mode selection (peaceful flight vs. Europe 1917 combat) rather than dynamic visual transitions. The simulation prioritizes authenticity of aircraft physics and real-world geography over complex faction or danger visualization systems. Safety and danger signals are primarily navigational (landmarks vs. terrain hazards) rather than narrative or environmental storytelling. The game establishes world-state through terrain layout (US cities vs. WWI grid-divided area) and available aircraft type rather than dynamic visual manifestation.
sources_used: wikipedia: Microsoft Flight Simulator (1982 video game)
